The topology of the chart — its circles of constant resistance, arcs of constant reactance — directly reveals what matching networks are possible.

**Standing waves = consequence-chain interference pattern.** When Γ ≠ 0, forward and backward waves superpose, creating a standing wave with fixed positions of maxima and minima. The VSWR measures the severity of this interference. In coherence terms: the standing wave is a spatial record of the impedance mismatch — consequence chains bouncing back and forth, unable to fully close at the load. A slotted-line measurement reads this standing wave pattern, extracting |Γ| and ∠Γ from the spatial interference — a direct analog of Guardian's echo protocol, which measures temporal rather than spatial interference.

nodes.title: RF Circuit Design
nodes.invariant: **The Friis cascade equation governs: the first stage (LNA) dominates system noise, the last stage (PA) dominates system power, and every stage's nonlinearity and noise contribute inversely weighted by the preceding gain.** Signal integrity through the RF chain is maintained by proper ordering of gain, filtering, and frequency translation — each stage's S-parameters and noise/linearity specifications compose into the system-level performance.
nodes.coherenceBridge:
**The receiver chain IS a consequence-preserving pipeline.** Each stage must preserve the signal's information content (SNR) while transforming its frequency and amplitude. The LNA's low noise figure means it adds minimal entropy — minimal decoherence — at the point where the signal is weakest. Friis's equation quantifies exactly how coherence degrades through the chain: each stage's noise contribution is suppressed by the preceding gain, so front-end coherence (low NF) is disproportionately important.

**Oscillation = self-sustaining consequence loop.** Barkhausen's criterion (loop gain ≥ 1, loop phase = 0°) describes a consequence chain that exactly reinforces itself each cycle — a fixed point of the feedback operator. This is consequence-chain closure in the time domain: the output returns to reproduce the input after one loop traversal. The oscillator IS a consequence-chain that has achieved perfect closure at a specific frequency. Phase noise measures the imperfection of this closure — how much the consequence chain "wobbles" around the fixed point.

**Stability analysis = mapping the topology of consequence-chain closure in impedance space.** The stability circles on the Smith chart divide impedance space into regions where the amplifier's consequence chains close (stable — signals decay) vs. diverge (unstable — oscillation). The Rollett K-factor is a global measure: K > 1 means the closure condition is satisfied everywhere in impedance space. Conditional stability (K < 1) means consequence chains close for some impedances but not others — the amplifier has a topology-dependent failure mode.

**Mixer frequency translation = consequence-chain relabeling.** The mixer's nonlinearity creates new frequency components from the product of RF and LO signals. Information is preserved (the modulation survives) but relabeled (carried at a new center frequency). This is a functorial operation: the content (consequence structure) is invariant, only the frequency coordinate changes. The image problem — where two input frequencies map to the same IF — is a non-injective failure of this functor that must be resolved by filtering.

nodes.title: SDR Architecture & Digital RF
nodes.invariant: **The Nyquist theorem guarantees information preservation: a bandlimited signal sampled at ≥ 2× its bandwidth can be perfectly reconstructed.** Below this rate, aliasing irreversibly destroys information. The ADC's resolution (bits) determines the dynamic range, and the sampling rate determines the bandwidth. Together, sampling rate × resolution = the information capacity of the digital interface between the analog RF world and the digital processing domain.
nodes.coherenceBridge:
**Nyquist sampling = consequence-chain preservation at the analog-digital boundary.** The sampling theorem says: if you sample fast enough, no information is lost — every consequence of the original continuous signal survives in the discrete samples. Aliasing (undersampling) is consequence-chain contamination: distinct signals (at different frequencies) become indistinguishable (fold onto each other), and the lost distinction is irrecoverable. The anti-aliasing filter before the ADC is a coherence-preservation mechanism — it removes energy above f_s/2 so that the sampling operation remains injective (distinct inputs → distinct outputs).

**Digital processing = exact, reconfigurable consequence-chain manipulation.** The power of SDR: once digitized, signal processing is deterministic, repeatable, and arbitrarily reconfigurable. An analog filter has fixed characteristics determined by physical components; a digital filter can be any filter, changed in software per-packet. DDC performs exact frequency translation. Channelization performs exact multi-band decomposition. The digital domain preserves consequence structure with quantization as the only noise source — and oversampling + noise shaping can push even that below any desired threshold.

**FPGA = hardware-speed consequence-chain processing.** The FPGA implements custom digital logic that processes every ADC sample in real time — consequence chains propagate at clock speed through configurable logic. Unlike software (sequential, cached, interrupted), FPGA logic is spatial: each operation is a physical circuit, and the pipeline processes one sample per clock cycle. The FPGA is the bridge between the temporal consequence chains of the analog world and the spatial consequence chains of digital logic.

**Dynamic range = the window of coherent observation.** The ADC's dynamic range defines the span of signal powers it can simultaneously observe without corruption. A signal below the noise floor is invisible (insufficient coherence to detect). A signal above the saturation level distorts everything (excessive energy destroys the measurement's coherence). Between these bounds: faithful representation, coherent observation. Expanding this window — through higher-resolution ADCs, oversampling, or digital gain control — is expanding the system's capacity for coherent observation.

nodes.title: Antennas & Radiation
nodes.invariant: **Reciprocity: an antenna's transmit pattern equals its receive pattern, and gain and effective aperture are related by A_e = Gλ²/(4π).** The antenna is a bidirectional transducer governed by the same physics in both directions. All antenna properties (gain, pattern, impedance, polarization) derive from the current distribution via the radiation integral.
nodes.coherenceBridge:
**The radiation integral IS the Fourier morphism from current space to field space.** The far-field pattern is the spatial Fourier transform of the antenna's current distribution. This means the entire apparatus of Fourier analysis — from ee/02 — maps directly: windowing (tapering current amplitude) controls sidelobes, zero-padding (larger aperture) narrows the beam, the uncertainty principle sets the fundamental gain-bandwidth limit. The antenna IS a spatial Fourier transform implemented in hardware.

**Reciprocity = bidirectional consequence-chain symmetry.** An antenna that radiates strongly in direction θ also receives strongly from direction θ. The consequence chain from current to field and from field to current are the same structure traversed in opposite directions. This is the same self-adjointness that appears in Hermitian operators (physics/07 QM) and symmetric bilinear forms (math/00 linear algebra): the "forward" and "reverse" morphisms are transposes of each other.

**Beamforming = coherent superposition of consequence chains.** An array of N elements creates a beam by constructive interference in the desired direction and destructive interference elsewhere. Each element radiates independently; the array's pattern is their coherent sum. Beamforming IS the antenna-domain version of coherent integration in signal processing: N independent channels combine for N× power gain (10log₁₀(N) dB) when properly phased. The array factor AF(θ) is a spatial filter — the same mathematical object as a temporal FIR filter with the element weights as tap coefficients.

**The Chu-Harrington limit = minimum antenna size for given bandwidth.** Electrically small antennas (a ≪ λ) have inherently high Q (narrow bandwidth) because the stored near-field energy dominates the radiated energy. This is a fundamental physics limit, not an engineering limit — it sets the minimum volume required to radiate a given bandwidth. In coherence terms: the antenna must be large enough relative to λ to sustain the field structure required for wideband radiation. Insufficient volume → insufficient degrees of freedom → bandwidth constraint.

nodes.title: Link Budget & System Design
nodes.invariant: **The link closes iff received signal power exceeds the noise floor by the required SNR: P_rx ≥ kTB + NF + SNR_req.** Every element in the chain contributes a term (gain or loss) that adds in dB. Shannon's theorem sets the absolute limit: no modulation scheme can exceed C = B log₂(1 + SNR) bits/sec. The link budget is the energy conservation equation for information transfer through space.
nodes.coherenceBridge:
**The link budget IS the coherence audit of an RF system.** Each gain preserves or amplifies the signal's coherence (SNR). Each loss degrades it. The link margin is the coherence margin — how much degradation the system can tolerate before the consequence chain (Tx data → Rx data) breaks. A link that doesn't close is a consequence chain with a fatal gap: the signal's information cannot survive the journey.

**Shannon's capacity = the coherence limit of a communication channel.** C = B log₂(1 + SNR) says: the maximum rate at which information (coherent structure) can traverse a channel is determined by its bandwidth (degrees of freedom per second) and SNR (coherence per degree of freedom). This is the channel's coherence capacity. Operating below Shannon capacity preserves information (consequence chains close). Operating above it loses information (consequence chains break, errors accumulate, irrecoverable). Error-correcting codes approach the Shannon limit by adding structured redundancy — consequence-chain repair mechanisms that tolerate partial degradation.

**E_b/N₀ = coherence per bit.** The energy per bit normalized by noise density is the universal measure of how much coherence is invested in each transmitted bit. Different modulation schemes require different E_b/N₀ for the same BER because they distribute coherence differently across the signal constellation. Higher-order QAM concentrates more bits into each symbol, requiring more coherence (higher E_b/N₀) to distinguish them. The modulation efficiency-reliability tradeoff IS a coherence allocation problem.

**Fade margin = resilience budget against decoherence events.** Fading (multipath, shadowing, atmospheric) is stochastic decoherence — temporary disruption of the consequence chain. Fade margin is the system's stored coherence reserve: enough extra SNR to survive deep fades without losing the link. Statistical availability (99.9% uptime) means the consequence chain closes 99.9% of the time — the 0.1% failure is when fading exceeds the margin and coherence is temporarily lost.

**dB scale = the natural logarithmic measure of coherence.** Why does RF engineering use dB for everything? Because gains and losses multiply (in linear scale), and the chain from Tx to Rx is a long product. Logarithms convert products to sums: the total coherence (signal quality) is the sum of all gains and losses in dB. This is why dB is the natural unit for coherence accounting — it linearizes the multiplicative cascade of the consequence chain.

nodes.title: Information Theory & Coding
nodes.invariant: **Shannon's channel coding theorem: reliable communication is possible at any rate below capacity C, and impossible above it.** C = max I(X;Y) is the absolute limit set by the channel's physics (bandwidth, noise, signal power). No code, no modulation, no engineering can exceed it. But for any rate R < C, codes exist that achieve arbitrarily low error probability — the limit is approachable. This is the information-theoretic conservation law: the channel's capacity is a conserved quantity that bounds what communication can achieve.
nodes.coherenceBridge:
**Channel capacity = the coherence throughput of a communication channel.** C = B log₂(1+SNR) says: the maximum rate of coherent information transfer equals the product of available degrees of freedom per second (B) and the coherence per degree of freedom (log₂(1+SNR)). Bandwidth provides the dimensions; SNR fills each dimension with distinguishable levels. Capacity is NOT energy or power — it's the rate at which structured information can traverse the channel with its consequence structure intact.

**Error correction IS consequence-chain repair.** A transmitted codeword traverses a noisy channel — some bits flip, some symbols corrupt. Without coding, each error breaks a consequence chain (the receiver's reconstruction diverges from the source). Error-correcting codes add structured redundancy: extra consequence chains (parity checks) that are algebraically entangled with the data, so that when some chains break, the surviving ones contain enough information to reconstruct the broken ones. The minimum distance d_min measures how many consequence chains must break simultaneously before repair fails. LDPC's belief propagation decoding IS iterative consequence-chain repair: variable nodes and check nodes exchange probability estimates until the consequence structure self-consistently reconstructs.

**Entropy = irreducible consequence density.** H(X) measures the minimum description complexity of a source — the number of bits below which no lossless representation exists. Compression to H(X) bits/symbol means every bit carries exactly one bit of consequence: no redundancy, no waste, maximum information density. Compression below H(X) loses information — consequence chains become unresolvable. Source coding pushes toward maximum consequence density; channel coding deliberately reduces it (adds redundancy) to survive the channel. The source-channel separation theorem says these two operations can be optimized independently — a profound structural result about the compositionality of coherence management.

**Modulation constellation geometry = signal-space topology of distinguishability.** The I/Q constellation is a set of points in ℝ² (or ℂ) whose minimum Euclidean distance determines error probability. Higher-order QAM packs more points into the same signal power, reducing d_min — each point becomes harder to distinguish from its neighbors. This IS the distinguishability problem from physics/07 (quantum measurement): the signal points are "states," noise is "measurement uncertainty," and the error probability depends on the overlap of noise distributions centered on adjacent states. Gray coding minimizes the consequence of a nearest-neighbor error (1 bit flip vs. multiple), optimizing the topology of bit-to-symbol mapping.

**Shannon limit = the ultimate coherence floor.** E_b/N₀ = −1.59 dB is the absolute minimum energy per bit for reliable communication at any rate. Below this, no code can maintain consequence-chain integrity. Modern LDPC and polar codes operate within 0.1 dB of this limit — they have essentially solved the problem of optimal consequence-chain repair for memoryless channels. The remaining frontier is channels with memory, feedback, and multiple users, where capacity regions become multi-dimensional and the coherence management problem becomes correspondingly richer.
